1: A shadow: "Sing to the LORD a new song." Revelation 5:9 — "they sang a new song: 'Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God.'" The new song is the song of the Lamb — sung because Christ has done the new thing that ends all exile and brings the new creation. The command to sing it to "all the earth" is the scope of the gospel.
2: A direct: "Proclaim his salvation from day to day." The word for "salvation" (yĕšûʿâ) is Jesus's name — Matthew 1:21 ("you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins"). The daily proclamation of God's salvation is the daily proclamation of the gospel of Jesus Christ. Acts 5:42 — "every day, in the temple and from house to house, they did not cease teaching and preaching that the Christ is Jesus."
3: A theme: "Declare his glory among the nations, his marvelous works among all the peoples." Matthew 28:19-20 — the Great Commission sends disciples to all nations to proclaim what God has done. Acts 1:8 — "you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth." The declaration of God's glory among the nations is the mission of the church, centered on the marvelous work of the resurrection.
4: A revelation of God: "the LORD is great and greatly to be praised; he is to be feared above all gods." Philippians 2:9-11 — "God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow." The greatness that surpasses all other claims to greatness is the greatness of Christ, the name above all names.
5: A revelation of God: "the gods of the peoples are idols, but the LORD made the heavens." Acts 17:22-31 — Paul's Areopagus sermon: "The God who made the world and everything in it is the Lord of heaven and earth." Colossians 1:16 — all things were created through Christ. The one who made the heavens is the one through whom all things were made — the son who is the agent of creation.
6: A revelation of God: "Splendor and majesty are before him; strength and beauty are in his sanctuary." Hebrews 1:3 — Christ "is the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature." Revelation 1:12-16 — the vision of the risen Christ in splendor and majesty. The splendor before God is most fully visible in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
7: A revelation of God: "Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength." Revelation 5:12 — "Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing!" The ascription of glory and strength to the Lord is the ascription of the heavenly worship to the Lamb — the praise that the psalm calls for is the praise that Revelation describes directed at Christ.
8: A type: "bring an offering and come into his courts." Hebrews 10:10,14 — Christ offered himself as the one sacrifice that ends all others. Through him we come into God's courts — Hebrews 10:19-22 ("we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus"). The offering we bring is no longer animal sacrifice but the "sacrifice of praise" (Hebrews 13:15 — "the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name").
9: A revelation of God: "Worship the LORD in the splendor of holiness; tremble before him, all the earth!" Hebrews 12:28-29 — "let us offer to God acceptable worship, with reverence and awe, for our God is a consuming fire." The splendor of holiness in which acceptable worship occurs is the holiness of Christ in whom we stand (1 Corinthians 1:30 — he became our sanctification). We worship in his holiness, not our own.
10: A shadow: "Say among the nations, 'The LORD reigns!' Yes, the world is established, it shall never be moved; he will judge the peoples with equity." Revelation 11:15 — "the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ." Acts 17:31 — God "has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed." The reign of the LORD is the reign of Christ.
11: A shadow: "Let the heavens be glad, and let the earth rejoice; let the sea roar, and all that fills it." Revelation 19:1-7 — the great multitude in heaven praising God: "Hallelujah! Salvation and glory and power belong to our God!" The cosmic joy that Ps 96 calls for is the eschatological joy at the final salvation that Rev 19 describes as already erupting in anticipation.
12: A shadow: "let the field exult, and everything in it! Then shall all the trees of the forest sing for joy." Romans 8:21-22 — "creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God." The creation that sings for joy is the creation liberated at the renewal of all things (Matthew 19:28; Revelation 21:5 — "Behold, I am making all things new").
13: A direct: "He comes to judge the earth. He will judge the world in righteousness, and the peoples in his faithfulness." Acts 17:31 — "he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed." Revelation 19:11 — "in righteousness he judges and makes war." The judge who comes is Christ, the appointed judge of all humanity. The whole Psalter's vision of divine judgment converges on his return.
